data.epi_content:
No matter how much healing magic you cast, a missing foot won't grow back.

Evan must know this, yet he continues to desperately recite spells with trembling hands.

I don't know how much magical power he poured in, but when blood started dripping from his nose, I simply pushed him away from me.

"It's fine, I'm used to it."

At least it's the left foot, which is somewhat fortunate.

I can still hobble around like a cripple.

Except for the opposite location, this is quite familiar so it's fine.

My arms are still intact too.

"This wasn't supposed to happen, wasn't supposed to... Am I still not good enough?

If I send you back one more time, just one more time, would that fix everything...?"

Evan mumbles to himself, repeating nonsense about sending me back.

I shake him and pat his cheeks, but he won't look at me, so I give him a light push.

But his body is so rigid that he doesn't budge at all.

"Evan, you won't die from a shoulder wound, so remove the bullet yourself."

I placed the gun barrel against Evan's shoulder and pulled the trigger.

That seemed to bring him back to his senses. He shook his head once, apologized, and embraced me.

With only one leg to balance on, I nearly fell backward, but somehow managed to stay upright.

I don't know what bizarre experiments he's done to his body, but his flesh regenerates on its own and even pushes out the embedded bullet.

"Evan, aren't you getting tired of all this?"

"I am tired, always tired. I'm exhausted beyond belief.

You just saw it yourself—fighting those things repeatedly is draining.

What makes it worse is that they seem like you can reason with them, but you absolutely can't, and compromise is completely out of the question."

Evan walks to the center of the room, irritably scratching his head.

"I've tried talking to them countless times.

I've offered to save you somehow, to turn back time—I even made such proposals when you died because of Vivian's mistakes.

When Vivian kills you, you're just dead. There's no concept of coming back like you and me."

"If killing one close friend could save ten strangers, would you do it? The person who points at me every time we meet, calling me a deranged, evil, cursed, horrible, death-deserving black magician, while claiming strangers whose faces and names they don't even know are more important than loved ones—isn't that person the truly insane one?"

Evan rarely talked about how difficult things had been for him, how tired he was, or how often his efforts had failed.

Whether he wanted to appear strong or didn't want to make me anxious, I'm not sure.

But I think sometimes it's necessary to put down the burden on your shoulders and take an irresponsible break.

Or just give up entirely.

That's why I gave up, but even my surrender failed. Haha.

"So it was hard for you?"

Listening to Vivian reminds me of those few pot-bellied men from old London talking about the greatest happiness for the greatest number.

Of course, it's not exactly the same, but she would choose the sacrifice of a few individuals if it meant happiness for a greater number.

"Yes. It was hard."

"What did you hate about it?"

"I hated having crazy people following me around."

"But wasn't there anything good about it?"

"When I stormed the Imperial Palace and slowly roasted that stupid Emperor—that was quite enjoyable.

I could see it—a way to save you was just within reach."
data.epi_content2:
Evan finally stops looking at my vanished left foot.

Though it was a terrible time, he looks at me while recalling some of the few positive moments, wearing a slightly smug smile.

But what he's really seeing is his imagination.

"If you went so far as to burn the Emperor alive, what was Vivian doing at that time?"

"She abandoned the Emperor and went to the northern Grand Duchy.

That pathetic fool was probably still spouting nonsense about making Vivian his concubine.

That's just the kind of man he was."

Evan chuckles to himself as he tells me about the Emperor—now the Crown Prince—burning to death.

It wasn't particularly pleasant, but when you're comforting someone, you don't mention that.

"Come to think of it, Vivian chose the greater number of people then too.

She remained in the Empire even when the Emperor was hitting on her.

So many people died that everyone in the Empire fled north or west, so Vivian went north too."

The Northern Grand Duke.

Hearing that makes the world suddenly feel like a bad joke.

Now this novel seems less like Vivian's love story and more like an adventure where she teams up with the handsome, capable, and silent Northern Grand Duke to hunt down Evan and me.

"Anyway, I've planted people in various places and prepared everything to cause chaos."

When I gave him a look that clearly asked when I had made such preparations—hadn't he been with me every day?—Evan cleared his throat and explained defensively.

"I wasn't just playing around at the Academy without making any preparations."

"Alright, take care."

I took out a handkerchief from my pocket and handed it to Evan.

He stared at the white handkerchief for a long time before taking it and tucking it inside his coat.

Then he smiled faintly, turned around, and started heading out.

"You can eat everything inside. I've prepared enough to last about two years.

If I don't return after three months, go down to the village on the right side of the hill slope for news.

If I don't return after a year..."

I interrupted Evan before he could finish.

I didn't want to hear it.

"Take care."

Evan silently nodded and finally left the room.

If there had been nothing in the room except food, I might have been quite bored, but fortunately, the walls were filled with books that seemed to exist solely for knowledge transfer, with no entertainment value whatsoever.

I looked through the books one by one, then stared at my empty left foot for a long time before lying down on the bed and closing my eyes.

Not that it made me fall asleep.

………………………

Three months passed quickly.

A life of eating, washing at a nearby stream, reading books all day, and sleeping becomes routine after the initial boredom wears off.

And Evan didn't return.

Didn't he say I should go down to the village to hear news?

But I really don't want to go down.

It's inconvenient to move without a leg, and it's cold.

………………………
data.epi_content3:
Six months have passed.

Evan still hasn't come.

He said to wait a year, so if I wait, he'll come.

Come to think of it, when I told him to take care, did he actually say he would return?

"I wonder when Evan will come back."

I asked while stroking my new pet—well, not really an animal since it's not alive and doesn't move, but it's convenient because it doesn't need food or water.

My neat-looking pet rock didn't answer.

I got bored with it and threw it toward the fireplace, but remembering how my palms had burned when I retrieved it last time, I threw it near the edge of the bed instead.

………………………

Nine months have passed.

I've read all the books in the room and now understand their contents perfectly, so I've started reading them again from the beginning for review.

I'm beginning to get a sense of how to use the black magic Evan mentioned.

………………………

A year has passed.

Unable to bear it any longer, I strapped my precious gun to my waist, loaded up on bullets, and left this tiny cabin.

Except for picking up my pet rock and going out to wash, I hadn't left for any other purpose, so it felt quite refreshing.

I followed the slope down as Evan had instructed and took the right path at the fork.

Soon, a small village that seemed to house about a hundred people came into view.

Looking at the faces of the villagers, they all appeared gloomy and sad.

I noticed there were no young men to be seen.

I stopped an old man passing by with an axe and asked:

"The village seems underpopulated. Has something happened?"

The old man, thinking I might be a nobleman... which I am, actually.

Anyway, he bowed deeply and answered.

"These days, they've taken all the able-bodied men from our village to hunt down that black magician.

But soon that cursed fellow will be defeated..."

The old man, happy to have someone to talk to after a long time, eagerly shared various details.

From what I gathered, Johannes had become Emperor, but Evan had captured him.

And now Evan was holed up in the Imperial Palace, holding out.

Soon, Vivian and her people would defeat him, though not immediately.

There was only one thing to do.

Unlike Vivian, everything around me was useless except for what I held dear.

Born a noble, I took it for granted, and even now in my fallen state, I don't deny it—so the life of the old man before me wasn't particularly important.

I pushed aside the old man who was insulting Evan and predicting his imminent death, took his axe, and brought it down on his chest.

A lowborn daring to insult a man of the Strelitz family—this much is acceptable.

Yes, it's fine.

I no longer want to remain alone, spinning in circles in this timeline.

Rather than being passively swept along by the currents of time, I've decided to go against the flow and take one step forward.

That day, though many fled, I split the heads of the powerless children, the elderly, and the women, and offered them as sacrifices while praying to the god.

Just as the book described, he whispered to me that he was pleased and gladly accepted them.
